 {"id":"bd-1hnyy","title":"Cost Attribution Custom Tags","description":"## Overview

User-defined tags on agents/sessions that flow to cost records, enabling custom slicing of cost data (by feature, sprint, team, experiment).

## Background & Justification

Cost tracking already has dimensions:
- Organization
- Project
- Agent
- Task
- Session

But these are fixed. Teams need custom dimensions:
- Feature (which feature is this work for?)
- Sprint (which sprint to bill?)
- Team (which team owns this cost?)
- Experiment (A/B testing cost comparison)

Custom tags enable project-specific cost analysis without code changes.

## Key Components

### 1. Tag Data Model

```typescript
interface CostTag {
  key: string;    // e.g., "feature", "sprint", "team"
  value: string;  // e.g., "auth-v2", "sprint-42", "platform"
}

// Tags can be set at multiple levels
interface TagScope {
  scope: 'agent' | 'session' | 'task' | 'manual';
  agentId?: string;
  sessionId?: string;
  taskId?: string;
}
```

### 2. Tag Propagation

Tags flow to cost records:
- Agent-level tags → all costs for that agent
- Session-level tags → costs during that session
- Task-level tags → costs for specific task
- Manual tags → applied retroactively

Inheritance: Agent > Session > Task (more specific wins)

### 3. Tag Management

**Setting Tags:**
- UI: Tag editor on agent/session detail
- API: Set tags on creation or update
- CLI: `--tags feature:auth,sprint:42`

**Tag Validation:**
- Key: alphanumeric + underscore, max 50 chars
- Value: any string, max 100 chars
- Max 10 tags per scope

### 4. Cost Analytics Integration

Extend cost queries:
```sql
-- Costs by feature
SELECT tag_value, SUM(cost_millicents) 
FROM cost_records 
JOIN cost_tags ON ...
WHERE tag_key = 'feature'
GROUP BY tag_value;

-- Compare experiments
SELECT 
  tag_value AS experiment,
  SUM(cost_millicents) AS total_cost,
  COUNT(DISTINCT agent_id) AS agents
FROM cost_records
JOIN cost_tags ON ...
WHERE tag_key = 'experiment'
GROUP BY tag_value;
```

### 5. UI Components

**Tag Editor:**
- Key-value input fields
- Autocomplete for existing keys
- Add/remove tags

**Cost Filter by Tag:**
- Tag key dropdown
- Tag value multi-select
- "Group by tag" option

**Tag-based Reports:**
- Cost by tag key chart
- Tag value breakdown table
- Tag trend over time

### 6. API Extensions

- `POST /api/agents/:id/tags` - Set agent tags
- `GET /api/agents/:id/tags` - Get agent tags
- `POST /api/sessions/:id/tags` - Set session tags
- `GET /api/cost-analytics/by-tag` - Query costs by tag
- `GET /api/tags/keys` - List used tag keys
- `GET /api/tags/values/:key` - List values for key

## Database Schema

```sql
CREATE TABLE cost_tags (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  cost_record_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  tag_key TEXT NOT NULL,
  tag_value TEXT NOT NULL,
  source_scope TEXT NOT NULL, -- agent, session, task, manual
  source_id TEXT,
  created_at TEXT
);

CREATE INDEX idx_cost_tags_key ON cost_tags(tag_key);
CREATE INDEX idx_cost_tags_key_value ON cost_tags(tag_key, tag_value);

CREATE TABLE tag_definitions (
  key TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  description TEXT,
  allowed_values TEXT, -- JSON array, null = any
  created_at TEXT
);
```

## Implementation Tasks

1. Design tag data model and schema
2. Add tag tables to database
3. Extend agent/session APIs with tag endpoints
4. Implement tag propagation to cost records
5. Extend cost analytics queries for tag filtering
6. Build tag editor UI component
7. Add tag filters to cost analytics UI
8. Create tag-based report views
9. Implement tag autocomplete
10. Write unit tests for tag propagation
11. Write integration tests for cost queries
12. Write E2E test for tag flow

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Tags can be set on agents, sessions, tasks
- [ ] Tags propagate to cost records automatically
- [ ] Cost analytics can filter/group by tags
- [ ] Tag autocomplete shows existing keys
- [ ] Reports break down costs by tag values
- [ ] Tag inheritance works correctly

## Dependencies

+{"id":"bd-1prjp","title":"[Maintenance] Wire SIGINT/SIGTERM to drain background jobs + exit","description":"## Scope

- Register process signal handlers (SIGINT/SIGTERM) to initiate drain mode.
- Stop accepting new work:
  - refuse new sessions/jobs
  - stop scheduling periodic cleanup loops
- Wait for drain completion (up to deadline), then exit.

## Notes

- Bun has different process lifecycle semantics than Node; implement with care.
- Prefer a single central place that owns all interval handles so we can stop them deterministically.

## Observability

- Log one structured "shutdown initiated" line and one "shutdown complete" line.
- Include counts: active WS connections, active jobs, pending job queue length.

## Acceptance

- Gateway exits cleanly under SIGINT/SIGTERM with bounded drain time.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-01T05:15:15.372876205Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T05:16:02.089640832Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["backend","ops"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1prjp","depends_on_id":"bd-1vdtm","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-01T05:15:15.390514814Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1prjp","depends_on_id":"bd-32lz3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-01T05:16:02.089595287Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

-{"id":"bd-1t3b4","title":"Hot Configuration Reload with Version History","description":"## Overview

Allow runtime configuration changes without restarting the gateway, with version history and rollback capability. This improves the operational experience by eliminating downtime for configuration updates.

## Background & Justification

Currently, configuration changes require a gateway restart. This is disruptive for:
- Tuning rate limits during high load
- Adjusting safety rule thresholds
- Updating cost alert thresholds
- Changing polling intervals

The config.service.ts already has a well-structured multi-layer config system (env → project → user → defaults). Adding hot reload is a natural extension.

## Key Components

### 1. Hot Reload Manager

Extend config.service.ts with:
- `setConfig(key, value)` - Apply config change immediately
- `reloadAll()` - Reload from all sources
- Config change event emission
- Validation before apply
- Rollback capability

**Categorize configs:**
- Hot-reloadable: rate limits, thresholds, timeouts, feature flags
- Restart-required: database URL, port, auth keys (marked clearly in UI)

### 2. Config Version History

Track every config change:
```sql
CREATE TABLE config_versions (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  config_key TEXT NOT NULL,
  old_value TEXT,
  new_value TEXT,
  changed_by TEXT, -- user or system
  changed_at TEXT,
  reason TEXT,
  version INTEGER
);
```

Features:
- Full history of changes per config key
- Diff between versions
- Attribution (who changed, why)
- Easy rollback to any version

### 3. Config Broadcast

When config changes:
- Emit event to all connected services
- WebSocket broadcast to connected clients
- Update in-memory cache immediately
- Log change to audit trail

WebSocket channel: `system:config`
```json
{
  "event": "config_changed",
  "key": "rate_limit.requests_per_minute",
  "oldValue": 100,
  "newValue": 150,
  "changedBy": "admin@example.com",
  "timestamp": "2025-..."
}
```

### 4. API Endpoints

- `GET /api/config` - Current config (filtered by permissions)
- `PUT /api/config/:key` - Update config value
- `GET /api/config/:key/history` - Version history for key
- `POST /api/config/:key/rollback` - Rollback to specific version
- `GET /api/config/hot-reloadable` - List of hot-reloadable keys
- `POST /api/config/reload` - Force full reload from sources

### 5. UI Components

- Config editor with syntax-aware input
- Hot-reloadable indicator (green = hot, orange = restart needed)
- Version history panel with diff view
- One-click rollback buttons
- Validation feedback before save
- Confirmation dialog for restart-required changes

## Implementation Tasks

1. Extend config.service.ts with hot reload capability
2. Add config_versions table to database schema
3. Implement version tracking on config changes
4. Add validation layer for config values
5. Implement rollback logic
6. Create WebSocket channel for config broadcasts
7. Add API routes for config management
8. Build config editor UI component
9. Build version history UI with diff viewer
10. Add audit logging for config changes
11. Write unit tests for hot reload logic
12. Write integration tests for version history
13. Write E2E test for config change flow

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Hot-reloadable configs apply immediately without restart
- [ ] Non-hot-reloadable configs clearly marked in UI
- [ ] Version history shows all changes with attribution
- [ ] Rollback restores previous value
- [ ] Invalid config values rejected with clear error
- [ ] WebSocket broadcasts config changes to clients
- [ ] Config changes logged in audit trail

## Technical Considerations

- Thread-safe config updates (Bun single-threaded, but async boundaries)
- Atomic config updates to prevent partial state
- Clear documentation of which configs are hot-reloadable
- Consider config dependencies (if A changes, must B change?)

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
- Config validation logic
- Version tracking
- Rollback logic
- Hot-reload categorization

### Integration Tests
- Config change propagation
- WebSocket broadcast
- Database persistence

### E2E Tests
- Change config via UI, verify immediate effect
- View history, perform rollback
- Verify restart-required warning","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:06:37.626061691Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T03:06:37.626061691Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

+{"id":"bd-1vdtm","title":"Graceful Shutdown, Drain Mode, and Maintenance State","description":"## Overview

Add first-class graceful shutdown and maintenance/drain mode to Flywheel Gateway so production deploys, restarts, and upgrades do not drop in-flight work, corrupt state, or strand WebSocket clients.

## Background / Why This Matters

Gateway runs long-lived WebSocket connections and background jobs (cleanup loops, ingest loops, health broadcasters, etc.). Today the process can exit at arbitrary points (SIGINT/SIGTERM, container eviction, deploy) with no explicit coordination:

- New requests can still be accepted while we are trying to stop.
- In-flight jobs may be interrupted mid-write.
- WebSocket clients may see abrupt disconnects with no actionable guidance.
- Operators have no safe "maintenance" mechanism to drain agents before making changes.

This feature makes restarts predictable and makes the system safer under real operational conditions.

## Goals

- Predictable shutdown behavior on SIGINT/SIGTERM.
- Explicit maintenance mode that:
  - refuses new mutating operations (spawn, run, approve, etc.)
  - optionally allows read-only routes
  - drains existing background work up to a deadline
  - provides machine-readable status for automation
- WebSocket clients receive a clear close reason and can reconnect appropriately.

## Non-Goals

- Multi-node leader election / distributed draining (single-process semantics first).
- "Zero downtime" across multiple replicas (this is a prerequisite, not the whole solution).

## Proposed Approach

1. Introduce a process-wide `ShutdownCoordinator` (in-memory) with states:
   - `running` -> `draining` -> `stopped`
   - includes `startedAt`, `deadlineAt`, and `reason`
2. Add an admin-only endpoint to enter/exit maintenance mode:
   - `POST /system/maintenance` { enabled: boolean, deadlineSeconds?: number, reason?: string }
   - `GET /system/maintenance` returns state + counters (active WS, active jobs, in-flight requests)
3. Integrate with:
   - job queues (stop accepting new jobs, finish current or cancel safely)
   - WebSocket hub (broadcast "maintenance" event; close with code + reason)
   - agent lifecycle operations (block new sessions if draining)
4. On SIGINT/SIGTERM:
   - flip state to `draining` (deadline configurable)
   - stop accepting new HTTP requests (or return 503 for mutating routes)
   - close WS connections gracefully
   - flush/finish background jobs
   - exit with clear log line + structured context

## Failure Modes / Safety

- If drain deadline passes, we should force-close remaining WS connections and exit with a warning log.
- If shutdown coordinator fails, default to safety: refuse new mutating ops.

## Test Plan

- Unit:
  - state transitions and deadline handling
  - route guard (mutating routes blocked during maintenance)
  - WS close code/reason mapping
- Integration:
  - start gateway, open WS, enter maintenance -> verify broadcast + close
  - enter maintenance -> spawn endpoint returns 503 with structured error
- E2E:
  - dashboard shows maintenance banner and disables destructive buttons

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] SIGINT/SIGTERM triggers drain mode and completes within configured deadline.
- [ ] New mutating operations are blocked during maintenance/drain.
- [ ] WS clients get a deterministic close code + reason and can reconnect.
- [ ] Status endpoint exposes drain progress and remaining time.
- [ ] Structured logs include correlationId/traceId and shutdown metadata.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-01T05:14:17.257144715Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T05:14:17.274572672Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["backend","ops","reliability"]}

+{"id":"bd-2lgg9","title":"Rate Limiting & Quotas (HTTP + WebSocket)","description":"## Overview

Make rate limiting a first-class, consistently enforced capability across **HTTP** and **WebSocket** entry points.

The codebase already has rate limiting primitives (middleware + tests), but enforcement is not uniformly applied and WS traffic is not governed by the same policy. This feature closes that gap.

## Goals

- Enforce sane defaults for public/unauthenticated and authenticated traffic.
- Provide per-scope policy support:
  - per-IP
  - per-user / per-workspace
  - per-API key
  - per-agent/session (where relevant)
- Ensure clients get deterministic feedback:
  - HTTP: `429` with `Retry-After` and `X-RateLimit-*` headers
  - WS: structured rate-limit events + close code for abusive clients

## Non-Goals

- Distributed/shared counters across multiple gateway replicas.
- Provider-side quota management (separate from BYOA rotation/failover).

## Proposed Approach

1. Audit current implementation:
   - `apps/gateway/src/middleware/rate-limit.ts`
   - config flags in `config.service.ts`
   - any per-route application
2. Apply middleware at the correct layer:
   - global defaults with explicit per-route overrides
   - exclude `/health` and other low-risk read-only endpoints
3. Define a stable keying strategy:
   - prefer user/workspace identity when auth is enabled
   - fall back to IP (with proxy-safe handling) when auth is disabled
4. Add WebSocket message rate limiting:
   - per-connection and per-auth-scope budgets
   - backoff hints for clients
5. Observability:
   - structured logs on rate-limit hits (info-level)
   - metrics counters (by route and scope)

## Test Plan

- Unit: key derivation, header formatting, limiter window resets.
- Integration: burst tests (concurrency) for several endpoints.
- E2E: UI behavior when API is rate-limited.
- Load: k6 scenario that verifies the limiter holds under high concurrency.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Rate limiting is enabled by default with documented configuration knobs.
- [ ] WS messages are governed by a limiter (no unbounded spam).
- [ ] Correct HTTP headers and `Retry-After` behavior.
- [ ] No regressions in existing rate-limit tests.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-01T05:16:48.847489564Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T05:16:48.873501653Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["backend","reliability","security"]}

+{"id":"bd-32lz3","title":"[Maintenance] Design shutdown coordinator + API contract","description":"## Goal

Define a concrete design for graceful shutdown + maintenance/drain mode:

- State machine (`running` / `draining` / `stopped`)
- Transition triggers (admin API, SIGINT/SIGTERM)
- Deadline semantics (hard cutoff vs best-effort)
- Error taxonomy for blocked operations
- WebSocket behavior (event, close code, reconnect hints)

## Deliverables

- Proposed module/API surface:
  - `ShutdownCoordinator` interface
  - `withMaintenanceGuard(...)` / middleware integration points
- Route spec:
  - `GET /system/maintenance`
  - `POST /system/maintenance`
  - response JSON shapes + OpenAPI updates
- Standard error codes (example):
  - `MAINTENANCE_MODE` (503)
  - `DRAINING` (503)
- WS close code + reason mapping (documented constants)

## Constraints

- Must respect existing auth model (admin-only when auth enabled).
- Must not introduce new long-lived background loops beyond what's needed.
- Must use structured logging (no ad-hoc console logs).

## Test Plan Notes

+{"id":"bd-9mcg1","title":"[Maintenance] Implement HTTP maintenance guard + status endpoints","description":"## Scope

- Implement `ShutdownCoordinator` (in-memory) per design.
- Add admin-only routes:
  - `GET /system/maintenance`
  - `POST /system/maintenance`
- Add a guard for mutating routes:
  - during maintenance/draining, return 503 with a stable error body
  - include `retryAfterSeconds` when a deadline exists

## Notes

- Prefer a middleware approach so we can apply policy consistently and avoid route-by-route drift.
- Exemptions:
  - `/health` should remain available
  - documentation endpoints can be configurable (default allow read-only)

## Logging

- On maintenance state change: log one structured line with { enabled, mode, deadlineAt, reason, actor }.
- On blocked request: log at `info` (not error) with route + auth subject.

## Acceptance

- Mutating endpoints are blocked during maintenance/draining.
- Status endpoint reflects current state and progress counters.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-01T05:14:50.847128606Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T05:15:43.654007400Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["backend"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-9mcg1","depends_on_id":"bd-1vdtm","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-01T05:14:50.864709306Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-9mcg1","depends_on_id":"bd-32lz3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-01T05:15:43.653976774Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

+{"id":"bd-ws55y","title":"[Maintenance] Implement WebSocket drain + maintenance broadcast","description":"## Scope

- On entering maintenance/drain:
  - broadcast a deterministic `system:maintenance` event on WS so clients can show a banner

  - if maintenance is enabled, clients should retry with backoff and display reason

  - `WS_CLOSE_MAINTENANCE`
  - `WS_CLOSE_DRAIN_DEADLINE`
- Ensure any WS backfill/replay logic does not spin when the server is draining.

  - connect WS -> enter maintenance -> observe broadcast then close
